one_line_takeaway: MIRA shows that reward hacking in inference-time diffusion alignment is best cured by an image-space, score-based KL constraint on the output distribution rather than by regularizing the input noise, winning ~80% of human head-to-head comparisons against the prior SoTA (DNO) while keeping distributional drift near zero.
key_finding: Directly regularizing the output image distribution, rather than the input noise, is what prevents reward hacking: across mechanism plots MIRA's drift stays near zero while DNO's drift grows with more optimization steps, and MIRA still wins the great majority of head-to-head comparisons despite achieving lower raw reward. Human evaluators prefer MIRA over the SoTA DNO ~4-to-1 (80.30% win rate) on SDv1.5, and MIRA-DPO preserves semantic content while optimizing a black-box JPEG-compressibility reward where DNO degrades the image to near-blank.
core_contribution: MIRA is a training-free, inference-time alignment method for text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models that mitigates reward hacking during noise optimization. Its central claim is that prior methods' noise-space regularization (bounding the perturbation of the input noise) is fundamentally insufficient, because the highly non-linear denoising map means tiny noise changes can produce large image changes (formalized as Proposition 1: closeness in noise space does not imply closeness in the induced image distribution). MIRA instead imposes an explicit image-space constraint: it augments the negative-reward objective with beta * KL[p_theta(x0|z,c) || p_theta(x0|z0,c)], penalizing divergence of the optimized-noise output distribution from the frozen base model's distribution. Since this KL is intractable in high dimensions, the paper's own derivation replaces it with a tractable upper-bound surrogate built from the model's diffusion score functions: sum_t sigma_t^2 (||s(x_t|z0,c)||^2 - ||s(x_t|z,c)||^2). A second contribution, MIRA-DPO, extends the framework to non-differentiable / black-box rewards by adapting Direct Preference Optimization to the frozen-backbone inference-time setting, using the same score-based surrogate to approximate the (otherwise intractable) log-likelihood-ratio reward.
threat_model: None. This is not a security paper and defines no adversary. 'Reward hacking' here is an optimization pathology, not an attack: gradient-based noise optimization over-optimizes an imperfect scalar reward (e.g., Aesthetic Score) and produces out-of-distribution images that score highly but ignore the prompt (their running example: prompting 'a fly' yields a high-aesthetic image that depicts no fly). The paper frames this as distributional drift to be regularized, not as a capability an attacker exploits, so no threat model is imposed.
limitations: Author-stated: MIRA adds moderate inference-time compute beyond standard DDIM/DDPM sampling; it relies on a principled score-based surrogate whose theoretical tightness is not formally analyzed and which may degrade on complex or out-of-distribution prompts; evaluation is focused on DDIM for compute parity though the objective is claimed sampler-portable. Reviewer-observed from the paper's own tables: not every comparison exceeds 50% win rate (e.g., MIRA vs DAS on Aesthetic Score is only 37.78%, and MIRA vs DyMO on Animal-Object Aesthetic is 46.53%), so the '>60% win rate' headline is a typical/median claim rather than universal. Win-rate judging leans heavily on GPT-4o and a single 100-person study, and much supporting detail (raw reward tables, CLIPScore, beta ablation) lives in the appendix beyond the extracted main text.

datasets_used: Simple Animals (45 prompts; used for drift/CMMD analysis)
Reward models/scorers: Aesthetic Score (LAION), HPSv2, PickScore (Pick-a-Pic), plus custom brightness/darkness and JPEG compressibility
quantitative_results: GPT-4o win rate MIRA vs DNO on Animal / Aesthetic: 80.00% (+/-2.22); on Animal-Animal Aesthetic: 77.27%; Animal-Object Aesthetic: 68.06%
Human study: MIRA preferred over DNO in 80.30% of comparisons on SDv1.5 (Aesthetic Score), 100 participants
Human study on SDXL: MIRA 55.75% win rate vs DNO; on SDXL-Turbo: 63.35% vs ReNO (HPSv2 objective)
Distributional drift (CMMD vs SDv1.5 reference, lower better): DNO 0.281 vs MIRA 0.063

Matched-wall-clock steering comparison (SDv1.5): MIRA vs FK Steering Aesthetic 57.78% / Darkness 75.56% / Brightness 73.33%; vs CoDe 55.56% / 73.33% / 77.78%; vs Best-of-N(50) 53.33% / 80.00% / 82.22%; vs DAS Aesthetic only 37.78% (Darkness 77.78%, Brightness 80.00%)
Peak GPU memory: MIRA constant 8.97 GB vs >20 GB for most steering baselines (FK 20.55, DAS 23.90, CoDe 24.51 GB)
Illustrative Aesthetic Scores for the 'fly' prompt: DNO 7.57 vs MIRA 5.53 (MIRA lower reward yet higher win rate / no hacking)
Reported win rates against base SDv1.5 reach ~90%
baselines_compared: DNO (Direct Noise Optimization) - primary SoTA baseline
